summary: O'Neil questions the true purpose of higher education after reading about failing for-profit colleges that enrich founders while saddling students with debt. She notes the proliferation of 'cash cow' MA programs at elite institutions like Columbia, observing that diversity concerns disappear when international students pay full tuition. Rather than accepting education's stated purpose of knowledge transmission, she offers eight cynical alternative explanations ranging from social networking to corporate training cost-shifting. The post suggests the education system has drifted far from its ostensible mission.
key_points: For-profit colleges exemplify education's corruption: high debt, poor outcomes, rich founders
Elite universities run 'cash cow' MA programs that abandon diversity principles for revenue
The knowledge transmission theory of education is 'probably the least likely option'
Education may function primarily as a ranking system for employers
Companies have shifted training costs onto workers via degree requirements as unions declined
money_quotes: It's enough to make you wonder what education is for.
And, whereas we 'care about diversity' in many of our college programs, in many of these they are utterly dominated by Chinese kids who get specific STEM versions of student visas.
It's a way to get these kids edumacated, for them to accumulate important knowledge. This is probably the least likely option.
It's a way to socialize our young people and help develop their networks and social skills, so that someday they can call on their friends to deploy insider trading.
It's a way to give day jobs to people who dedicate their time to gaming the US News & World Report college ranking model.

It's a way for companies to avoid training their workers and force them to pay for training before they get offered a job, now that unions have been decimated and nobody stays with a company for more than a few years.
key_insight: Higher education has evolved from knowledge transmission into a complex system serving institutional revenue, employer convenience, and social sorting—with actual learning as perhaps its least important function.
